lens.included_activities: Independent US iron and steel open-die, impression-die, press, hammer, and seamless rolled-ring forging firms in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band repeatedly producing qualified forged components for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive forging departments, steel mills without outsourced forging programs, custom roll formers, nonferrous forgers, firms manufacturing primarily proprietary finished products, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring engineered business-to-business programs priced per part, weight, or lot, with tooling, setup, heat treatment, testing, finishing, steel surcharges, purchase orders, releases, and multiyear qualification or supply agreements.

a.bridge: BLS measures national forging-machine occupations and broader fabricated-metal employment, not wage-weighted current task exposure in 332111. Heating, material handling, press or hammer operation, die work, trimming, heat treatment, inspection, maintenance, and finishing were treated as largely physical, while estimating, process-document support, scheduling, quality analysis, maintenance knowledge, procurement, and administration were partly exposed; this may understate engineering work in highly qualified forgings.
a.rationale: Forging depends on heavy equipment, hot material, dies, skilled setup, manipulation, maintenance, and testing. AI can assist information and diagnostic workflows but cannot replace most plant-floor tasks without physical automation and substantial capital.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from independent steel forgers by open-die, impression-die, and ring-rolling process, documenting current press, manipulator, furnace, inspection, ERP, and engineering automation.
caveats: Occupation evidence is national and not specific to 332111.
The estimate excludes conventional mechanization or robotics unless AI directly enables labor substitution.

rho.rationale: Quote retrieval, routing drafts, production and furnace scheduling, defect analysis, quality documentation, maintenance retrieval, purchasing, and administration can be implemented within five years. Safety, legacy controls, sparse failure data, customer qualification, metallurgy, export controls, traceability, and human sign-off constrain deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Audited forging AI deployments reporting labor hours, quote cycle time, yield, energy per part, downtime, first-pass quality, false defect calls, customer requalification, and human-review requirements.
caveats: No representative LMM implementation study was found.
Operator employment decline can result from mechanization and should not be attributed wholly to AI.

e.rationale: Independent steel forgers generally provide recurring, qualified external manufacturing and fit the operating lens. Captive facilities, proprietary-product producers, extreme customer concentration, equipment or furnace condition, environmental liabilities, weak management depth, and size-band misclassification remove a substantial minority.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the frozen n population showing captive status, external revenue, forging process, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, equipment and furnace condition, certifications, tooling ownership, environmental liabilities, and management depth.
caveats: No observed eligibility denominator was found.
The injected n=40 is an ESTIMATE using a machinery EBITDA margin and may misclassify steel forgers.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner ages across responding employer businesses rather than five-year control transfers for steel forgers. A portion of the 51% age-55-plus population plus ordinary strategic sales is translated into qualifying transfers after excluding internal succession, continued ownership, closure, and nontransferable assets; the small buyer universe may cause overstatement, while strategic scarcity may cause understatement.
s5.rationale: General owner aging creates succession pressure and qualified capacity can be strategically valuable. High transaction size, capital needs, environmental diligence, customer approval, cyclicality, and the very small estimated pool reduce completed-transfer probability.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of completed, failed, and withdrawn US steel-forging transactions in the EBITDA band, paired with owner-age, succession, customer-consent, environmental, and capital-plan interviews.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and data year 2018.
The number of potential buyers and sellers is small, making annual deal flow lumpy.

q.rationale: Qualification, tooling, metallurgical know-how, scarce press capacity, and high switching costs can protect gains from yield, energy, uptime, lead time, and quality. Steel surcharges, open-book programs, customer cost-down demands, and rebids still share some benefits over five years.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and program-margin sample showing steel and energy pass-through, open-book costing, cost-down clauses, qualification and tooling ownership, renewal cadence, and margin persistence after productivity changes.
caveats: No contract-level retention dataset was found.
Retention varies between defense or aerospace qualifications and price-competed automotive or industrial programs.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all fabricated metal products, not 332111 forged quantity, to grow 0.8% annually through 2034; AISI reports upstream steel shipments rather than forgings. Applying a flat five-year central path may understate defense, aerospace, energy, and reshoring demand or overstate it if automotive, imports, machining, casting, or additive processes displace forgings.
d5.rationale: BLS projects fabricated-metal output rising from $303.5 billion in 2024 to $329.5 billion in 2034, while AISI reports 2025 steel shipments up 5.1%. Forging-machine employment is projected down 18.9%, signaling productivity and process change; balanced against cyclical end markets and imports, the central quantity case is flat.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year North American steel-forging shipment forecasts by process and end market, reconciled with customer awards, vehicle, aerospace, defense and energy builds, imports, material substitution, and announced capacity.
caveats: Upstream steel shipments are not forging demand.
Broad fabricated-metal real output can diverge from the small steel-forging segment.

o.rationale: Forged steel components require accountable billet selection, heating, deformation, die or tool control, heat treatment, testing, traceability, and delivery. Software can improve decisions and equipment can automate manipulation, but neither removes the qualified manufacturing operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly internalizing forging, alternative processes eliminating forged components, or autonomous forging plants operating without accountable metallurgy, production, maintenance, and quality management.
caveats: Manufacturing-process substitution affects quantity and is captured mainly in d5.
Self-service ordering can remove transaction labor without removing the accountable forger.

narrative.executive_view: Iron and steel forging is a durable, qualified physical manufacturing service with moderate labor intensity but a very small estimated LMM pool. AI is useful around quoting, process records, quality analytics, maintenance knowledge, scheduling, and administration rather than the hot-forming operation itself.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can retrieve analogous jobs, assist estimates and work instructions, optimize schedules, summarize inspection trends, prioritize maintenance, and draft records. Heating, manipulation, hammer or press operation, die work, trimming, heat treatment, testing release, and repair remain physical or human-supervised.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification, tooling, scarce press capacity, and switching costs help retain yield, energy, uptime, and delivery gains. Steel surcharges, cost-down clauses, open-book costing, and rebids return part of recurring savings.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates only 40 firms in the band and uses a machinery margin bridge. Captive status, qualified programs, environmental obligations, equipment, customer concentration, capital plans, and stand-alone management determine the transferable pool.
narrative.demand_durability: Defense, aerospace, transportation, energy, and industrial replacement support forged parts, while imports, cycles, and process substitution create downside. Broad fabricated-metal growth and current steel shipments support a flat central five-year quantity case despite falling forging-machine employment.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest gaps are six-digit task and shipment data, current automation, customer program mix, imports, pricing terms, environmental liabilities, equipment condition, and true EBITDA. The small population and high customer concentration make aggregate estimates fragile.
narrative.principal_driver: Qualified repeat programs and scarce forging capacity support durable operator demand and retention of less visible yield and reliability gains.
narrative.principal_weakness: A tiny capital-intensive population with predominantly physical labor limits scalable AI substitution and transferable-firm volume.

sources.title: NAICS 332111: Iron and Steel Forging
sources.supports: Census defines iron and steel forging and notes that establishments may perform surface finishing such as cleaning and deburring on their forgings.

sources.title: Forging Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders, May 2023
sources.supports: BLS reports 9,170 forging-machine setters, operators, and tenders nationally in 2023 and defines their work as setting up, operating, or tending machines that taper, shape, or form parts.

sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS reports 8,800 forging-machine setters, operators, and tenders in 2024 and projects 7,200 in 2034, an 18.9% decline.

sources.title: Iron and Steel Manufacturing Effluent Guidelines
sources.supports: EPA identifies forging among regulated iron and steel manufacturing processes alongside hot forming, heat treatment, pickling, descaling, surface cleaning, and finishing.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older; estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects fabricated-metal-product real output increasing from $303.5 billion in 2024 to $329.5 billion in 2034, a 0.8% annual rate, while employment declines 3.9%.

sources.title: AISI Releases Revised 2025 Steel Shipment Data
sources.supports: The American Iron and Steel Institute reports full-year 2025 steel shipments of 91,158,528 net tons, up 5.1% from 86,698,917 in 2024.
